original_abstract: The article deals with the history of formulation of the great Balling formula and derivation of alcohol factors and attenuation quotient of which the different values are found in Balling books from 1846 and 1854. It is reported a modifi ed Balling formula, allowing directcalculation of original extract from the apparent extract and alcohol without the use of formula by Tabarie. The attenuation quotient q isuseful for simultaneously growing craft breweries. The value of Balling formula is to describe the relationship between fermented andresidual extract using a single measurement. This procedure has remained in stable form for over 150 years and it can not be assumedthat the technical practice applies a modifi ed version. It is proposed to specify both value of the real extract and alcohol for the consumerfi rst assessment of the nutritional value of beverage.
humanized_abstract: ALC Open Access DOI: 10.15303/AOCSANS.1077 (c) by Author(s) under CC-BY-NC-ND licensed originally published in Alimentaria Open Access The history of the Balling formula, the alcohol factors and attenuation quotient together with their different values provided in the old Balling books from 1846 and 1854 are described. A modifi ed version of the Balling formula that allows the calculation of original extract directly from apparent extract and alcohol through the formula of Tabarie is presented. The attenuation quotient “q” is especially useful for adjunct brewers, and especially for the case of the simultaneously brewed product which is a common method in some craft breweries. In any case, the advantage of the Balling formula is the fact that it enables by only one measurement to know the relationship between the fermented and the unfermented extract. A method that, for over 150 years, that has not undergone, so far as we know, any modifications, except possibly at the level of a possible modified version for an unknown particular case that has not come to our knowledge. It is also considered important to provide for the fi rst time in the history of beverages, both the real extract and the real alcohol for that at least initial evaluation by the consumer about the nutritional content of the beverage.
